4: Cited sparsely but pointedly in insect growth physiology. Wigglesworth's classic text invokes 'Przibram's rule' — that a body part should increase at each moult by the same ratio as the whole body — while Abercrombie cites the growth work in order to reject it; the rule and its refutation both trace back here.
9: Cited across a century of regeneration research (1927–2023), now mostly in molecular and developmental biology. Modern reviews of the salamander blastema credit Weiss among those who established that muscle, connective tissue and bone regenerate through the blastema; in his own day Przibram debated whether the blastemas Weiss called 'nullipotent' were in reality totipotent.
10: A tightly focused footprint in the 1920s–50s salamander-regeneration literature. Poležajew, Schwidefsky and Waechter all cite Weiss's 1927 demonstration that the regenerate's epidermis derives from the old skin and can differentiate on its own — the result that settled where the new skin comes from.
11: Cited into the present (1928–2021) in regeneration and stem-cell-adjacent work. Hellmich's contemporary account credits Weiss with the 1927 experimental proof that the new epidermis derives from the old, and the paper's potency-testing frame anticipates the modern blastema-potency question.
13: Cited by Przibram's own 1924 follow-up for the finding that lowering a rat's body temperature shortens its relative tail length — a building block of the institute's temperature-and-proportion program.
21: Cited into the present (to 2024) in butterfly colour-plasticity work and in the history of science. Modern studies invoke Brecher's principle that cues at the pupation site set pupal colour, while Hartenstein's history notes Brecher's 1923 bid to become the University of Vienna's first female scientist.
27: Cited by Vrtelówna, who notes that Koppányi had published directly contradictory findings on the eye-pupils of Bombinator — which prompted her own re-examination.
36: Cited in the 1920s–50s amphibian-transplantation literature. A French study describes Weiss grafting limbs onto same-species hosts and the supernumerary limb coming to move in homologous synchrony with the host, while Przibram engaged Weiss's blastema-potency claims directly.
37: Cited through the 1920s–50s for its transplantation results and their interpretation. Przibram questioned whether Weiss's 'nullipotent' blastemas were really totipotent, Hubl later rejected the Weissian 'morphogenetic field' in favour of form-building substances, and Przibram's handbook paired the work with Detwiler's.
38: The most-cited paper in the corpus here, drawn on from 1918 to the present (2026) across reproductive medicine, endocrinology and the history of sex science. Citers return to Steinach's puberty-gland transplants and his 'artificial hermaphrodites' — animals surgically given both testes and ovaries — that alternately expressed male and female characters.
39: A touchstone of the inheritance-of-acquired-characters debate, cited from 1911 to 2024. Modern historians and biologists — Vargas, Gliboff, Müller, Meyer, Weissmann, Arntzen — return to Kammerer's claim that male midwife toads pass acquired nuptial pads to the fourth generation, the result at the centre of the Kammerer affair and its epigenetics-era reappraisal.
42: Cited in the colour-change literature, notably by Kammerer, who drew on Secerov's result that exposing larvae to coloured backgrounds while the definitive pigment is still developing yields the same colour states — a parallel between regeneration and ontogenesis.
44: Cited continuously from 1904 to 2022 in evolutionary biology and the history of the axolotl and Salamandra. Contemporaries (Kaufman, Schultz, Grochmalicki) engaged Kammerer's induced-birth and intra-uterine-nutrition findings in fine detail; modern work (Reiß, Arntzen) treats it as a reference point — and a contested one — in the organism–environment and inheritance debates.
47: Cited by Schultz as a case — alongside Clavellina and Planaria — of reduction followed by regeneration after injury, then still histologically unexamined.
48: Cited from 1906 to the present (2026) across regeneration and evolutionary work. Przibram credited it with forcing the distinction, among 'adult' specimens, between those that have reached the species' usual size and those that have not — a distinction bearing on regenerative capacity.
49: Cited from 1906 to 2023 in crustacean biology and the study of bilateral asymmetry. Emmel engaged Przibram's heterochely work directly, quoting his theoretically grounded skepticism toward a reported case of bilateral 'crusher' claws.
51: A fully source-verified footprint. Kopeč and Megušar cite Werber's 1905 result on Tenebrio antenna-and-eye regeneration as concordant with their own, Przibram's mantis study compares the operation timing, and Kříženecký uses it to argue the heteromorphoses stem from destruction of the optic ganglia rather than amputation.
53: Cited by a 2012 study as among the first to document — and to characterize as generally poor — regenerative ability in the lancelet Amphioxus.
57: Cited from 1907 to 2022 in the midwife-toad and inheritance literature. Vargas's epigenetic reappraisal and Gliboff's critical history both return to Kammerer's reproduction experiments, the latter scrutinizing the reported breeding and backcrossing dates.
59: The seed of the institute's mantis program, cited across the 20th century. Przibram's own 1909 and 1912 sequels build on its protocols (more moults at higher temperature; the colour of the offspring), and Megušar (1910) confirms its conjecture that a mantis larva can regenerate its raptorial leg once it has completed enough moults.
60: A theoretical paper with a long, sporadic afterlife (1907–2011). Notably Spemann engaged its crystal-analogy idea — that regenerating a distal part from a proximal wound surface requires an inversion of the directing structure in the boundary layer.
63: Cited by Przibram's own 1921 paper, which extends the temperature-coefficient program from the cold-blooded mantis to a warm-blooded animal.
66: Cited by Brecher (1924), who found her results on antenna-feet agreeing with Przibram's 1917 findings on homeotic antenna-regenerates — a thread in the institute's homeosis work.
67: Cited in pigment physiology from 1919 to 2009. Przibram drew on Brecher's finding that temperature above 37° produces red Pieris markings by oxidation of the chromogens, and later workers (Schaeffer on tyrosinase, Yamanaka on hormonal pupal-colour control) cite it as a foundation.
69: The origin of the nerve-dependence-of-regeneration line. Murray and Bilski cite it in the 1920s, and Singer's mid-century work — which established the nerve threshold for regeneration — repeatedly cites Weiss's result as the foundational observation, as does Hauser (1965).
70: One of the institute's most enduring results, cited from 1925 to 2023 and now central to molecular limb-regeneration work. Contemporaries (Hertwig, Brunst, Liosner, Schwidefsky) engaged Weiss's humerus-extirpation proof that an organ's regeneration is not the sum of its tissues'; modern axolotl studies — including the landmark demonstration that blastema cells remember their tissue of origin — cite it among the classical foundations.
71: Fully verified and reaching into modern regeneration theory. Gräper and Abolinš engaged Weiss's 'whole-regenerate from a half cross-section' in the 1920s–30s, and Bryant (1974) cites it, with Goss, for the principle that a complete regenerate can form from half a limb stump — support for the regulative view of the limb.
73: Cited in Hydra biology from 1926 to 2001. Mutz made Burt's study a central reference of her transplantation work, and Burnett and Tardent cite his 'unipotent systems' analysis for the axial organization of the polyp.
81: A methodological touchstone of colour-change physiology (1926–1971). Healey and Ross continue Abolín's drug experiments on the minnow's background response directly, and Waring's synthesis of vertebrate melanophore control cites them among the evidence for autonomic regulation of colour change.
86: Cited from 1912 to 2022 and a central exhibit in the Kammerer case. Gliboff analyses it closely, while modern workers (Sanchez & Pröhl, Arntzen) treat the fire-salamander colour-pattern result as the one Kammerer claim partially confirmed by replication — though only its within-generation, environmentally induced part.
88: Cited through the mid-century salamander-transplantation literature. Stone's heteroplastic eye-transplant studies and Wachs's lens-regeneration work cite Uhlenhuth's demonstration that a transplanted organ keeps pace with the host's metamorphosis.
92: Cited from 1913 to 2023 in cave biology and vision science. Barr's synthesis on cave-animal evolution and Crescitelli's review of visual pigments both cite Kammerer's experiments on the olm's reproduction, eyes and body.
93: A foundation of crustacean colour-change endocrinology, cited from 1913 to 2011. Knowles, Perkins and Passano cite Megušar's experiments among the early demonstrations linking colour change to moulting and, later, to hormonal control.
98: Cited in the eye-transplantation and neural-specificity literature. Sperry — whose optic-nerve regeneration experiments founded the chemoaffinity hypothesis — cites the BVA eye-transplant work, as do Harrison and Stone, who treat it as a methodological precedent.
116: Cited in pigment biochemistry from 1912 to 1969, notably by Bloch, whose work on the DOPA reaction and skin-pigment formation cites Winkler's study among the contemporary efforts to explain how pigment forms.
119: Cited from 1909 to 2025 as the experimental heart of Kammerer's Lamarckian program. Gliboff's account of the affair treats it as central, and Baedke's organism-centered biology cites it as a historical case.
133: Cited in the foundational limb-axis literature. Harrison's analyses of symmetry and axis-reversal in transplanted limbs, and Butler's proximo-distal reversal experiments, all cite Kurz's study of the newt's leg-forming potencies.
135: The institute's quantitative-growth flagship, cited from 1913 to 2026. Modern growth-and-plasticity studies use its per-instar mantis measurements, and Coen's history of fin-de-siècle Viennese precision science treats them as a case study in quantification.
148: Cited across neuroscience and regeneration (1923–2015). Lashley's foundational paper on neural mechanisms in behaviour cites Weiss's transplanted-limb coordination — the seed of Weiss's 'resonance principle' of nerve–muscle specificity.
156: Cited from 1919 to 2024 as a model for environmentally cued colouration. Henke's firebug work, Biedermann's integument physiology and Bloch's 'Das Pigment' all cite the Przibram–Brecher Pieris experiments among the foundations of how environment shapes pigment.
164: Cited in reproductive endocrinology from 1920 to 2023. Moore's gonad–pituitary work and Hisaw's relaxin discovery cite Steinach's artificial-hermaphrodite-gland experiments for the principle of hormonal sex control.
165: Cited from 1920 to 2018 in the literature on the interstitial gland and ageing. Rössle's treatise on growth and ageing and Priesel's and Wiesner's gonad studies cite Steinach's histological constitution of the germ gland.
174: Cited in limb-duplication research (1922–1999). Bohn's grafting analysis of supernumerary triple legs and Swett's double-limb experiments cite Przibram's survey, and Shelton adopts his term 'Bruchdreifachbildungen' for naturally occurring triple-claw abnormalities.
50: A standard early reference for vertebrate regeneration. Werber's demonstration that the agile lizard can restore jaw tissue is cited from T. H. Morgan's Regeneration (1907) onward as a comparative case defining the scope of vertebrate regenerative capacity — taken up by Singer on nerve-induced limb regeneration and by Goss on lower-jaw regeneration in newts, and still invoked in Alibardi's modern reviews of reptile regeneration — a thread running from the institute's regeneration survey into present-day work on which vertebrates can regrow what.
129: Cited in the early regeneration literature as a key result that sexually mature insects can restore excised wings: Megušar (1907, 1910) compares Werber's beetle-wing findings with Kammerer's on the fly, while Kopeč (1913) and Kříženecký (1914) cite the 1907 result as a striking exception to the general rule of declining regenerative power with maturity.
130: Cited alongside Werber's beetle-wing work in the early debate on insect regenerative capacity — Megušar (1910) sets Kammerer's result on wing regeneration in the fly (Musca) against the poorer regenerability of the cockroach Stylopyga.
94: Now drawn on as an early precedent for transgenerational epigenetics: Aucamp's (2016) review of the biological significance of transgenerational epigenetic inheritance cites this paper as part of Przibram and Steinach's “somatic induction” research — an antecedent of today's concept of germline-independent soma-to-germline information transfer.
107: Survives in growth biology as the origin of “Przibram's rule.” Wigglesworth's textbook Growth (1972) invokes the rule — that a body part increases at each moult by the same ratio as the whole body — discussing it under allometric (heterogonic) growth as presupposing “harmonic growth.”
